literal: Yao said, “Raise altogether noble kin and distant hidden ones.”
idiomatic: Yao said, “Search out everyone, near kin or far, known or hidden.”

literal: The multitude all spoke to Yao, saying, “There is one unwed among the people, called Yu Shun.”
idiomatic: The assembly told Yao, “Among the commoners there is a bachelor named Shun of Yu.”

literal: Yao said, “Indeed, I have heard of him.
idiomatic: Yao said, “I have heard of him.”

literal: What of his person?”
idiomatic: What sort of man is he?”

literal: The Peaks said, “He is a blind man’s son.
idiomatic: The Peaks answered, “He is the son of a blind father.

literal: The father is stubborn, the mother vicious, the younger brother arrogant, yet he can harmonize them with filial piety; he lifts them to good order so that wickedness does not enter.”
idiomatic: The father is brutish, the mother spiteful, the half-brother overbearing, yet Shun has made peace in the house through filial duty and brotherly love, so that no evil takes root.”

literal: Yao said, “I will try him.”
idiomatic: Yao said, “Then I shall test him.”

literal: Thereupon Yao gave him his two daughters in marriage to observe his virtue toward the two women.
idiomatic: Yao therefore married his two daughters to Shun to see how he treated them.

literal: Shun lowered the two daughters to the bend of Guirui and they observed the rites of wives.
idiomatic: Shun settled the princesses on the south bank of the Guirui, and they kept every rule of a wife’s conduct.

literal: Yao approved him and thereupon made Shun carefully harmonize the five norms; the five norms obeyed.
idiomatic: Pleased with Shun, Yao set him to put the five relationships in order, and they fell into harmony.

literal: He then everywhere entered the hundred offices; the hundred offices were timely in order.
idiomatic: He next sent him through every ministry, and each office kept its proper season.

literal: He received guests at the four gates; the four gates were solemn, and feudal lords and distant guests all showed reverence.
idiomatic: He stationed him at the four outer gates; all was dignity, and lords from afar came as guests in awe.

literal: Yao sent Shun into the mountains and forests amid violent wind, thunder, and rain; Shun walked and was not lost.
idiomatic: Yao sent him into hills and marshes in howling wind and driving rain; Shun never lost his way.

literal: Yao deemed him sage and summoned Shun, saying, “You plan affairs and your words can be carried out three years.
idiomatic: Seeing his sagacity, Yao called Shun and said, “Three years you have planned the work of state and every word has borne fruit.

literal: Ascend the imperial seat.”
idiomatic: Mount the throne.”

literal: Shun yielded on account of virtue and was not pleased.
idiomatic: Shun declined out of modesty and would not presume to accept.

literal: On the first auspicious day of the first month Shun received the abdication at Wen Temple.
idiomatic: On an auspicious day in the first month Shun received the great charge in Wenzu’s temple.

literal: Wen Temple is Yao’s great ancestor.
idiomatic: Wenzu was the temple of Yao’s supreme ancestor.

literal: Thereupon Emperor Yao being aged, he commanded Shun to act as regent for the Son of Heaven’s government in order to observe Heaven’s mandate.
idiomatic: Yao had grown old, so he ordered Shun to act as regent and govern as Son of Heaven while heaven’s favor was read.

literal: Shun then used the pearl-adorned armillary and the jade traverse to align the seven regulators.
idiomatic: Shun aligned the seven luminaries with the pearl-sphere and jade sight-tube.

literal: Then he classified offerings to High Heaven, performed the yin rite to the six honors, sacrificed to mountains and rivers, and distinguished the host of spirits.
idiomatic: He ranked sacrifices to High Heaven, offered smoke to the six tutelary powers, looked toward the great mountains and streams, and sorted the lesser gods.

literal: He gathered the five tokens, chose an auspicious moon and day, met the Four Peaks and the shepherds, and distributed the tokens.
idiomatic: He assembled the five regalia, chose lucky days, convened the Four Peaks and regional lords, and handed out the tallies.

literal: In the second month of the year he toured east to Mount Dai, burned offerings, and sacrificed to mountains and rivers in their ranks.
idiomatic: Every second month he toured east to Tai Shan, kindled the great fire sacrifice, and offered to the peaks and rivers in due order.

literal: Then he met the eastern lords, harmonized seasons, months, and days, unified pitches, measures, weights, and balances, refined the five rites, the five jades, the three silks, the two live and one dead gifts for guests; as with the five regalia, when finished he returned them.
idiomatic: He then greeted the eastern chiefs, set calendar and seasons in accord, unified pitch-pipes, bushels, and balances, polished the five courtesies and the jade tokens, silks, and guest-gifts of live animals; when the rites ended he restored the regalia.

literal: In the fifth month he toured south.
idiomatic: In the fifth month he toured the south.

literal: In the eighth month he toured west.
idiomatic: In the eighth month he toured the west.

literal: In the eleventh month he toured north: all as at first.
idiomatic: In the eleventh month he toured the north—each circuit followed the same pattern.

literal: Returning, he reached the temple of the founding and distant ancestors and offered a bull with the special rite.
idiomatic: On his return he reached the shrines of founder and distant ancestors and sacrificed a bull with the highest rite.

literal: Every five years one tour; the hosts of lords attended court four times between tours.
idiomatic: He toured the realm once in five years, and the lords presented themselves at court on the four years between.

literal: Everywhere he announced commands with words, clearly tested merit with deeds, and with chariots and robes rewarded achievement.
idiomatic: He proclaimed his orders in speech, tested officers by their deeds, and rewarded merit with carriages and robes.

literal: He began the division into twelve provinces and opened the rivers.
idiomatic: He first carved the realm into twelve provinces and dredged their streams.

literal: He patterned punishments on the model statutes, by ban pardoned the five punishments, with the whip made office punishments, with the rod made instructional punishments, with metal made ransom punishments.
idiomatic: He took the standing statutes as models for punishments, used exile to commute the five corporal pains, appointed the lash for official faults, the rod for moral instruction, and fines of metal for redeemable crimes.

literal: For oversight calamities and oversights he pardoned.
idiomatic: Accidents and first faults he forgave.

literal: For those who stubbornly ended in villainy he applied punishment.
idiomatic: Habitual evil he punished without mercy.

literal: Revere, reverence—only let punishments be quiet.”
idiomatic: Be vigilant, be reverent—see that justice stays measured and calm.”

literal: Huan Dou spoke well of Gonggong; Yao said he could not be used, yet tried him as master of works, and Gonggong indeed ran to excess.
idiomatic: Huan Dou praised Gonggong; Yao refused the post but still tested him as minister of works, and Gonggong proved wanton and corrupt.

literal: The Four Peaks raised Gun to govern the vast flood; Yao deemed it impossible, but the Peaks urgently begged to try him; having tried him he had no success, so the hundred surnames found it inconvenient.
idiomatic: The Four Peaks urged Gun to master the flood; Yao doubted him, yet at their insistence gave him trial; Gun failed, and the people suffered for it.

literal: The Three Miao in the Yangzi and Han region and Jingzhou repeatedly made rebellion.
idiomatic: The Three Miao tribes along the Yangzi, Han, and Jingzhou rose again and again.

literal: Thereupon Shun returned and spoke to the emperor, asking to banish Gonggong to Youling to change the northern Di.
idiomatic: Shun then reported to Yao and asked to exile Gonggong to Youling to chasten the northern Di.

literal: To banish Huan Dou to Chongshan to change the southern Man.
idiomatic: He banished Huan Dou to Chongshan to awe the southern Man.

literal: To move the Three Miao to Sanwei to change the western Rong.
idiomatic: He removed the Three Miao to Sanwei to humble the western Rong.

literal: To execute Gun on Mount Yu to change the eastern Yi: with these four crimes all under Heaven submitted.
idiomatic: He slew Gun on Mount Yu’s slopes to warn the eastern Yi—with these four punishments the realm submitted.

literal: Yao seventy years on the throne obtained Shun; twenty years more he grew old and ordered Shun to act as regent for the Son of Heaven’s government and presented him to Heaven.
idiomatic: Seventy years Yao reigned before he found Shun; twenty years later, enfeebled, he made Shun regent and commended him to heaven.

literal: Yao yielded the seat twenty-eight years altogether and then died.
idiomatic: Twenty-eight years after yielding the throne Yao died.

literal: The hundred surnames were sorrowful as if mourning father and mother.
idiomatic: The people mourned as though they had lost their own parents.

literal: For three years within the four quarters none raised music, thinking on Yao.
idiomatic: For three years no music sounded within the four seas, so deep was the grief for Yao.

literal: Yao knew his son Dan Zhu was not worthy and insufficient to receive all under Heaven; therefore he weighed and handed the realm to Shun.
idiomatic: Yao knew his son Dan Zhu was unworthy and could not receive the realm, so he weighed the matter and transferred the mandate to Shun.

literal: Had he given it to Shun, all under Heaven would have gained the benefit and Dan Zhu alone would have suffered;
idiomatic: To give the throne to Shun would bless the world yet wound Dan Zhu alone;

literal: had he given it to Dan Zhu, all under Heaven would have suffered and Dan Zhu alone would have gained the benefit.
idiomatic: to give it to Dan Zhu would torment the world while only Dan Zhu profited.

literal: Yao said, “In the end I cannot cause all under Heaven to suffer for the sake of one man,” and finally he gave all under Heaven to Shun.
idiomatic: Yao declared, “I will never harm the myriad people for one man’s sake,” and in the end he ceded the empire to Shun.

literal: When Yao died and the three years’ mourning were finished, Shun yielded to Dan Zhu south of the southern river.
idiomatic: After Yao’s death and the three years’ mourning, Shun yielded the throne to Dan Zhu south of the southern river.

literal: The feudal lords who came to court did not go to Dan Zhu but to Shun; litigants did not go to Dan Zhu but to Shun; singers did not sing of Dan Zhu but of Shun.
idiomatic: Lords who attended court went to Shun, not Dan Zhu; plaintiffs sought Shun, not Dan Zhu; ballad-makers praised Shun, not Dan Zhu.

literal: Shun said, “It is Heaven,” and afterward entered the central domain and mounted the Son of Heaven’s seat; this is Emperor Shun.
idiomatic: Shun said, “This is heaven’s choice,” entered the central plain, and took the throne—Emperor Shun.

literal: Yu Shun was named Chonghua.
idiomatic: Shun of Yu was styled Chonghua.

literal: Chonghua’s father was Gu Sou; Gu Sou’s father was Qiaoniu; Qiaoniu’s father was Gouwang; Gouwang’s father was Jingkang; Jingkang’s father was Qiongchan; Qiongchan’s father was Emperor Zhuanxu; Zhuanxu’s father was Changyi: thus to Shun seven generations.
idiomatic: Chonghua’s father was Gu Sou, whose father was Qiaoniu, whose father was Gouwang, whose father was Jingkang, whose father was Qiongchan, whose father was Emperor Zhuanxu, whose father was Changyi—seven generations from Shun to Zhuanxu.

literal: From Qiongchan to Emperor Shun all were humble commoners.
idiomatic: From Qiongchan down to Emperor Shun the line had sunk to commoners.

literal: Shun’s father Gu Sou was blind; Shun’s mother died; Gu Sou remarried and begot Xiang, who was arrogant.
idiomatic: Shun’s father Gu Sou was blind; Shun’s mother had died; Gu Sou remarried and fathered Xiang, a proud and overbearing son.

literal: Gu Sou loved the later wife’s son and always wished to kill Shun; Shun fled and hid.
idiomatic: Gu Sou doted on the second wife’s child and often plotted Shun’s death; Shun slipped away.

literal: When he had small faults he received punishment.
idiomatic: When charged with petty faults he bore the blame.

literal: With reverence he served father, stepmother, and younger brother; daily he grew more earnest and never slackened.
idiomatic: Yet he served father, stepmother, and brother with growing devotion and never slackened.

literal: Shun was a man of Jizhou.
idiomatic: Shun came from the Ji region.

literal: Shun ploughed on Mount Li, fished in Thunder Marsh, potted by the Yellow River, fashioned tools at Shouqiu, and sought a livelihood at Fuxia.
idiomatic: He ploughed Mount Li, fished Thunder Marsh, threw pots along the Yellow River, forged tools at Shouqiu, and hired out his labor at Fuxia.

literal: Shun’s father Gu Sou was stubborn, the mother vicious, the younger brother Xiang arrogant; all wished to kill Shun.
idiomatic: His father remained brutish, his stepmother spiteful, his brother Xiang arrogant; all three still schemed against his life.

literal: Shun obeyed and fitted himself and did not lose the way of a son; he was fraternal and filial.
idiomatic: Shun stayed filial and brotherly and never broke a son’s duty.

literal: When they wished to kill him they could not get him.
idiomatic: When they tried to kill him they could never catch him.

literal: When they sought him he was always at their side.
idiomatic: Whenever they looked for him he was still at hand to serve.

literal: At twenty Shun was famed for filial piety.
idiomatic: At twenty his filial name was known everywhere.

literal: At thirty Emperor Yao asked who could be employed; the Four Peaks all recommended Yu Shun, saying he would do.
idiomatic: At thirty, when Yao asked for worthy men, the Four Peaks together named Shun of Yu.

literal: Thereupon Yao gave him his two daughters in marriage to observe his conduct within, and sent nine sons to dwell with him to observe his conduct without.
idiomatic: Yao therefore married his two daughters to watch Shun’s inner life and sent nine sons to live with him and watch his outer conduct.

literal: Shun dwelt at the bend of Guirui; his inner conduct grew daily more careful.
idiomatic: Shun lived on the Guirui bend and daily refined his private virtue.

literal: Yao’s two daughters did not dare to rely on their nobility to slight Shun’s kin; they were very observant of a wife’s way.
idiomatic: The two princesses never flaunted their rank over Shun’s family but kept every wifely duty.

literal: Yao’s nine sons all grew more honest.
idiomatic: Yao’s nine sons grew steadier under Shun’s influence.

literal: When Shun ploughed Mount Li, the men of Mount Li yielded him the boundaries.
idiomatic: When he ploughed Mount Li, the hill-folk yielded him the wider furrows.

literal: When he fished Thunder Marsh, the men on Thunder Marsh yielded him dwelling sites.
idiomatic: When he fished Thunder Marsh, the shore-dwellers gave him the better stands.

literal: When he potted by the Yellow River, the vessels by the river were without flaw.
idiomatic: When he threw pots on the Yellow River, no potter along the bank turned out cracked ware.

literal: In one year the place he dwelt became a village; in two years a town; in three years a capital.
idiomatic: Within a year his hamlet swelled to a village, in two to a town, in three to a city.

literal: Yao thereupon gave Shun fine linen, a zither, built him granaries, and gave him cattle and sheep.
idiomatic: Yao then gifted him fine cloth and a zither, built granaries for him, and presented oxen and sheep.

literal: Gu Sou still again wished to kill him; he made Shun mount the high granary while Gu Sou from below set fire to the granary.
idiomatic: Gu Sou still plotted murder: he sent Shun to roof the high granary, then fired the stacks from below.

literal: Shun then used two bamboo hats to shield himself and came down, went away, and did not die.
idiomatic: Shun opened two wide bamboo hats like wings and floated down, escaping the flames.

literal: Afterward Gu Sou again made Shun dig a well; Shun dug the well and made a hidden side tunnel to come out.
idiomatic: Later Gu Sou ordered him to dig a well; Shun cut a secret lateral tunnel and climbed out.

literal: When Shun had gone deep, Gu Sou and Xiang together filled the well with earth; Shun came out through the hidden tunnel and went away.
idiomatic: When Shun had gone deep, Gu Sou and Xiang dumped earth down the shaft; Shun slipped through the side passage and vanished.

literal: Gu Sou and Xiang rejoiced, taking Shun for already dead.
idiomatic: Father and brother rejoiced, sure Shun was dead.

literal: Xiang said, “The plot was mine.”
idiomatic: Xiang said, “The scheme was my idea.”

literal: Xiang with his father and mother divided the spoils, and then said, “Shun’s wives—Yao’s two daughters—and the zither are mine.
idiomatic: Xiang divided the booty with his parents, saying, “Shun’s wives, Yao’s daughters, and his zither belong to me.

literal: The cattle, sheep, and granaries I give to father and mother.”
idiomatic: The herds and granaries go to you.”

literal: Xiang then entered Shun’s palace and dwelt there, strumming his zither.
idiomatic: Xiang marched into Shun’s quarters, took the zither, and played.

literal: Shun went in and saw him.
idiomatic: Shun walked in on him.

literal: Xiang startled was not pleased and said, “I was thinking of you, brother, and was depressed.”
idiomatic: Xiang jolted, sullen, said, “I was brooding on you, elder brother, and strumming out my sorrow.”

literal: Shun said, “Yes, you are indeed my younger brother.”
idiomatic: Shun answered gently, “Yes, you are still my brother.”

literal: Shun again served Gu Sou, loved his younger brother, and grew daily more careful.
idiomatic: Shun afterward served his father and cherished his brother with even greater care.
